claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children I'm asking about - Alex who's 5, Blake who's 17, and Charlie who's 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19).

Let me ask about each of them to determine eligibility:

Let's start with **Blake, who is 17**. Since Blake is over 16, their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training. Is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training at the moment. They finished school but haven't gone on to college or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake.

Now let me ask about **Charlie, who is 19**. Is Charlie currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is still studying. They're doing their A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Charlie.

Now I need to ask about your living situation and responsibility for the children. Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. All three of them live with me here in the UK. We're in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. 

Just to confirm - are you the person responsible for all three children (meaning you provide for their care, food, clothes, etc.), and is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for all three of them - I provide for their care, food, clothes, all of that. And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess eligibility for each of your children.

Let me evaluate each child's situation:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

**✓ Alex (age 5) - ELIGIBLE**
Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for their care. You qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

**✗ Blake (age 17) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Blake is over 16 and is not currently in approved education or training. For children aged 16 and over, Child Benefit only continues if they're under 20 and in approved education or training. Since Blake has finished school and hasn't gone on to college or training, you would not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

**Note:** If Blake registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks.

**✓ Charlie (age 19) - ELIGIBLE**
Charlie is under 20 and is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). You qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie as long as they continue in their approved education.

**Summary:** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for **2 out of your 3 children** - Alex and Charlie.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, which is under 16. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK and is responsible for their care. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex meets all the criteria for eligibility.

Blake is 17 years old. For children aged 16 or over, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training program. Blake also has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks after leaving education). Therefore, Blake does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

Charlie is 19 years old and is currently studying A-levels at sixth form college. According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. A-levels at sixth form college qualify as approved education. The claimant lives with Charlie in the UK, is responsible for their care, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie meets all the criteria for eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Leeds) and is responsible for three children. Alex (5) qualifies as they are under 16. Blake (17) does not qualify because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Charlie (19) qualifies because they are under 20 and currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college).

According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex meets all the criteria for eligibility.

Blake is 17 years old. For children aged 16 or over, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training program. Blake also has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks after leaving education). Therefore, Blake does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

Charlie is 19 years old and is currently studying A-levels at sixth form college. According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. A-levels at sixth form college qualify as approved education. The claimant lives with Charlie in the UK, is responsible for their care, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie meets all the criteria for eligibility.

Blake (17) does not qualify because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Charlie (19) qualifies because they are under 20 and currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college).
